summary:
The Wabanaki Confederacy (c. 1680s–present) was an alliance of Algonquian-speaking peoples of northeastern North America — the Abenaki, Mi'kmaq, Maliseet, Passamaquoddy, and Penobscot nations — who formed a political and military alliance to resist English colonial expansion into their territories in New England and Acadia. The Confederacy emerged in the context of the colonial wars of the late 17th century, particularly after [[event:3267|King Philip's War]] (1675–1676), which had decimated the Wampanoag and allied peoples to the south and demonstrated the catastrophic cost of individual resistance to English expansion. By forming a broader coalition, the Wabanaki were able to sustain resistance through multiple conflicts — Dummer's War (1722–1725), King George's War (1744–1748), and the French and Indian War (1754–1763).

The Wabanaki's political effectiveness was enhanced by their alliance with New France — French missionaries had established deep relationships with Wabanaki communities, and the Wabanaki raided English settlements repeatedly during the various colonial wars, often in coordination with French strategic objectives. The fate of the Confederacy was determined by the outcome of the broader imperial struggle: the French defeat in the [[event:2456|French and Indian War]] and the subsequent British control of Canada removed the French alliance that had sustained Wabanaki resistance. Land cession treaties through the late 18th and early 19th centuries reduced Wabanaki territories to reservations in Maine, New Brunswick, and Nova Scotia, though Wabanaki nations maintained legal personality and negotiated the Maine Indian Claims Settlement Act (1980) — one of the largest Indigenous land settlements in US history.
